concept: The Formula for Anger — A = ET + S
content:
The formula for Anger is universal. It doesn't matter the language, race, religion or culture, we all get angry in the exact same way. We can put this in an equation that will work with everyone on the planet:
A = ET + S
Anger = Egocentric Thinking + Should
references: IB4: Shoulds, Musts, and Demands (Chapter 4); The ABCs of How Emotions are Created (Chapter 3)

content: Egocentric thinking is the concept of 'I'-ness. In other words, we place ourselves at the center of the universe; 'Anything I don't like, find unpleasant, inconvenient, frustrating, or annoying, should not exist. Anything I do like should spring into existence!' This is crazy thinking! Whether or not we like something does not change its existence. You are not the center of the universe and the universe is not here to bend to your every wish. Your opinion of the situation has absolutely no bearing on its existence. Egocentric thinking alone doesn't create Anger. It only sets us up for it.
references: The Formula for Anger — A = ET + S; Mental Contamination (Chapter 1); IB7: The Idea of Entitlement (Chapter 4)

concept: S: Should — Its Role in Creating Anger
content: We only get angry when we say to ourselves a Should, Must or Demand. Should is a constricting concept, which means that it cannot be proven and leads to misery. A freeing concept is something that can be proven and leads to a mind of peace, a mind that doesn't struggle. A more freeing concept than Should (a demanding philosophy) is a desiring philosophy, such as 'it would be better…,' 'it would be preferable…,' 'I would have liked if so and so happened or didn't happen.' This we can prove or disprove. This is what makes it a science—it has a way to be measured. It is evidence-based. Should implies a moral obligation and demand that simply does not exist in nature. We only get angry when we insist that reality should be different than how it is, right now! In its extreme form, we call this psychosis—the denial or the non-acceptance of reality. When we are angry, we are all a little bit psychotic because we are saying that what exists should not exist just because we don't like it.
references: The Formula for Anger — A = ET + S; IB4: Shoulds, Musts, and Demands (Chapter 4); Resistance to Reality (Chapter 3)

content: When you should on yourself, you get angry at yourself and you put yourself down. When you should on others, you get angry at them and put them down. You may be as correct as you want to be about how poor their behavior was, but not being able to control your Anger won't benefit you or them or the situation. Here at The Parr Institute we like to think of should as a four-letter word, because it is as useless and harmful as any profanity and leads you to being angry. Should is the number one irrational belief to always be red-flagging. There is no emotional disturbance without a Should, Must, or a Demand!
references: IB4: Shoulds, Musts, and Demands (Chapter 4); Red-Flagging (Chapter 4); The Formula for Anger — A = ET + S

concept: Acceptance Does Not Mean Endorsement — Key Distinction
content: Acceptance does not mean endorsement! You do not have to approve of poor behavior in order to be happy, but you do want to accept that people are fallible and make mistakes often. Unpleasant things happen to everyone on a regular basis—you and I are no exception. I am not saying to endorse cheating or any other behavior we may define as bad, but usually when something like that happens, we insist that it should not have happened. This is irrational, because it already did happen. Insisting that it must not be the way it already is, is what causes the negative emotion. So if you want to feel better from the negative emotion, find a way to accept and embrace what did happen, even if it's something very unpleasant.
references: Acceptance (Chapter 3); IB4: Shoulds, Musts, and Demands (Chapter 4); The Formula for Anger — A = ET + S

concept: Three Reasons People Behave Poorly — Framework
content:
There are only three reasons people do things that are harmful or unproductive:
1: Diminished Mental Capacity — Some people through no fault of their own simply do not have the intellectual capacity to understand how to do things that are in their own best interest. It does not do well to judge or berate someone for behavior that results from diminished mental capacity. The only truly appropriate emotional reaction on your part is compassion and acceptance of the situation as it is.
2: Ignorance — Sometimes we say or act in a certain way that inconveniences ourselves or others simply because we were not aware of all of the conditions. When someone doesn't know how to do the thing that is in their or your best interest, the appropriate emotional reaction on our part is again compassion. It is possible for them to change with knowledge and experience, but that too is out of your control.
3: Disturbance — If someone is acting poorly and they are not stupid or ignorant, there is the only one other reason they might do something that is not in their best interest—disturbance! It has to be disturbed thinking on their part. We have already proven that a negative emotion is psychosis; when someone is feeling intense negative feelings, their physical brain and their thinking mind is not functioning properly. The appropriate emotional reaction from you to a person's poor behavior from disturbance is again compassion. You cannot change or alleviate someone else's disturbed thinking. Their disturbance is entirely out of your control. The only disturbance you can control is your own.
The poor behavior of others is not something we can control, regardless of its source. The only rational response to all three is compassion.
references: The Formula for Anger — A = ET + S; Acceptance Does Not Mean Endorsement; IB1: 'It' Statements (Chapter 4)

content: There is only one rational Should: everything that happens today should happen! All others can be disputed. When you feel angry, first ask yourself, 'Where is my Should, Must, or Demand?' Second, 'Why should (or shouldn't) it be this way? Why shouldn't it be the way someone else likes? Why shouldn't it be exactly as it is?' If you are actively red-flagging your Shoulds, it will be easy to see your Anger coming and begin to dispute it by asking yourself specific questions about your belief. When you feel angry, find and observe the activating event, and then red-flag your SMDs. There will always be a Should, Must, or Demand! Finally, dispute the statement you red-flagged. This is using your conscious mind rather than your reactive mind.
references: IB4: Shoulds, Musts, and Demands (Chapter 4); The 4 Steps to Disputing (Chapter 4); The Four Challenge Questions (Chapter 4); Red-Flagging (Chapter 4)

concept: Case Example — Bank Overdraft Fee (Anger Formula Applied with 4 Steps)
content:
Applying the Formula for Anger and The 4 Steps to Disputing to a bank overdraft situation:
A = You find a $30 overdraft fee on your bank statement.
B = 'I should not have been charged this fee!'
C = You are angry and your account is still $30 short.

STEP 1: OBSERVE — What physiological sensations are you experiencing? Face got hot, heart began to race. Actions: stomped into the bank, crumpled bank statement.

STEP 2: SOS — What am I thinking about myself, others, or the situation? You don't like the bank charge because it's inconvenient, so you shouldn't have been charged! The bank or teller should have made an exception for you. 'I am entitled to better treatment—because I am special!'

STEP 3: IDENTIFY — Belief: You should not have been charged. Truth: You were charged; it's right there in black and white! The conditions existed for it to happen—1) the bank put in place a monetary penalty for spending more money than you have in your account, and 2) you spent more than was in your account—therefore it should have happened.

STEP 4: REPLACE — Replace irrational, demanding statements with preference statements or desiring philosophies. 'That idiot teller should have given me back my money!' can become, 'It sure would have been nice if the bank cut me a break.' It's okay to have preferences, but it is when our preferences become Shoulds, Musts and Demands that we get emotionally disturbed.
references: The Formula for Anger — A = ET + S; The 4 Steps to Disputing (Chapter 4); IB4: Shoulds, Musts, and Demands (Chapter 4)

concept: Case Example — Ripped Shirt, Late Phone Call (Should Replacement Illustrations)
content:
Examples of replacing Should statements with desiring philosophies in everyday Anger situations:
• 'She should not call so late at night.' — Well, she should have because she did, although it might have been better or more convenient for you if she didn't.
• 'He must not cheat on me.' — Must is a demand word, and again, although it's much preferable for you if he did not cheat on you, there is no law in the Universe that says he must not do what he wants to do or what he does.
• 'I ripped my shirt and it shouldn't have ripped and now I'm really pissed!' — How did your shirt piss you off? The shirt has no emotion nor any direct connections to emotions (that's A-to-C thinking). When you look for the B, the belief that your shirt shouldn't rip because you don't want it to, you can replace that with, 'It would have been a lot more convenient if my shirt didn't rip, but it did.'
references: S: Should — Its Role in Creating Anger; IB4: Shoulds, Musts, and Demands (Chapter 4); A-to-C Thinking (Chapter 3)
